word_list.word: blind men can judge no colors
word_list.pronunciation: blˈaɪnd mˈɛn kæn dʒˈʌdʒ nˈoʊ kˈʌlɚz

senses.definition: used to imply that someone who lacks knowledge or experience in a particular area cannot make accurate judgments or opinions about it

examples.x: As someone who has never experienced parenthood, he cannot understand what it's like to raise a child; blind men can judge no colors.

word_list.word: judge not, that ye be not judged
word_list.pronunciation: dʒˈʌdʒ nˈɑːt ðæt jiː biː nˌɑːt dʒˈʌdʒd

senses.definition: used to advise people to avoid being critical or judgmental of others, as they may be judged in the same manner themselves, encouraging compassion and understanding instead

word_list.word: the devil is not (so|as) black as he is painted
word_list.pronunciation: ðə dˈɛvəl ɪz nˌɑːt sˌoʊ æz blˈæk æz hiː ɪz pˈeɪntᵻd

senses.definition: used to suggest that things or people are often not as bad as they seem or as they are portrayed
used to suggest that comparing people or things is often unproductive and can lead to negative feelings or judgments

examples.x: "Comparisons are odious," said Jane, refusing to engage in a debate about which of her two friends was more successful.

word_list.word: fools and bairns (should|shall) never see (a|) half done work
word_list.pronunciation: fˈuːlz ænd bˈɛɹnz ʃˌʊd ɔːɹ ʃˌæl nˈɛvɚ sˈiː ɐ ɔːɹ hˈæf dˈʌn wˈɜːk

senses.definition: used to imply that unfinished work should not be shown to inexperienced or uninformed individuals, as it may lead to negative perceptions or incorrect assumptions about the final outcome

examples.x: The contractor instructed his crew to complete the construction project before inviting the clients for a walkthrough, as he didn't want them to see the unfinished work - after all, fools and bairns should never see half done work.

word_list.word: there are two sides to every question
word_list.pronunciation: ðɛɹˌɑːɹ tˈuː sˈaɪdz tʊ ˈɛvɹi kwˈɛstʃən

senses.definition: used to imply that individuals should be open-minded and consider different perspectives before making a judgment or decision

examples.x: In a court of law, both the prosecution and the defense have the opportunity to present their case, as there are two sides to every question.

word_list.word: every horse thinks its own pack is (the|) heaviest
word_list.pronunciation: ˈɛvɹi hˈɔːɹs θˈɪŋks ɪts ˈoʊn pˈæk ɪz ðə hˈɛviəst

senses.definition: used to imply that people tend to believe that their problems, responsibilities, or burdens are more difficult than those of others, and may lack empathy towards others' struggles

examples.x: Sarah insists that her job is the most difficult in the company, but she needs to remember that every horse thinks its own pack is heaviest.

word_list.word: first impressions are the most lasting
word_list.pronunciation: fˈɜːst ɪmpɹˈɛʃənz ɑːɹ ðə mˈoʊst lˈæstɪŋ

senses.definition: used to suggest that the way people perceive someone or something during their initial encounter can have a strong and lasting impact on their opinion

examples.x: John made sure to be on time for his first day at work since he knew that first impressions are the most lasting.

word_list.word: (one|a) swallow does not make a summer
word_list.pronunciation: wˈʌn ɐ swˈɑːloʊ dʌznˌɑːt mˌeɪk ɐ sˈʌmɚ

senses.definition: used to suggest that a single positive event or piece of evidence is not enough to establish a pattern or draw a definitive conclusion

word_list.word: hatred is (just|) as blind as love
word_list.pronunciation: hˈeɪtɹᵻd ɪz dʒˈʌst æz blˈaɪnd æz lˈʌv

senses.definition: used to imply that intense emotions, whether they be love or hatred, can cloud one's judgment and make one unable of seeing things objectively

examples.x: The two countries were so consumed by their mutual hatred that they failed to see the benefits of working together, proving that hatred is as blind as love.

word_list.word: whoever writes a book, (should|shall) be ready to accept criticism
word_list.pronunciation: huːˈɛvɚ ɹˈaɪts ɐ bˈʊk ʃˌʊd ʃˌæl biː ɹˈɛdi tʊ ɐksˈɛpt kɹˈɪɾɪsˌɪzəm

senses.definition: used to suggest that those who create or present their work to the public should be willing to listen to criticism and feedback and use it to improve the quality of their work

examples.x: The writing instructor told the students that whoever writes a book should be ready to accept criticism, and encouraged them to revise their work based on feedback from others.

word_list.word: (you|) show me the man, and I will show you the rule
word_list.pronunciation: juː ʃˈoʊ mˌiː ðə mˈæn ænd aɪ wɪl ʃˈoʊ juː ðə ɹˈuːl

senses.definition: used to imply that a person's behavior reflects their character, emphasizing the ability to make judgments about a person based on their actions

word_list.word: do not judge a man until you have walked (a mile|) in his shoes
word_list.pronunciation: duːnˌɑːt dʒˈʌdʒ ɐ mˈæn ʌntˈɪl juː hæv wˈɔːkt ɐ mˈaɪl ɔːɹ ɪn hɪz ʃˈuːz

senses.definition: used to encourage empathy and understanding by emphasizing that an individual should not judge or criticize someone's situation or behavior without experiencing it themselves
